section_title: INTRODUCTION TO ICT CHARTER PRICE ACTION MODEL 1: INTRADAY SCALPING
content:
,560
We're looking at the first of 12 price action models, and this is going to be specifically dealing with intraday
scalping. ICT price action model number one: Intraday Scalping
- Previous Day High and Low.

section_title: PREREQUISITE AND TRADER PROFILE FOR MODEL 1
content:
,879
There's a prerequisite to this tutorial: you should have watched the 'Mastering High Probability Scalping
Volume 1 through 3' tutorial. This was made public as part of my free tutorials on my website and YouTube
channel.
The trader profile used for this model is one that we're hypothetically using, and this trader is hypothetically:
- Not willing to hold long-term or overnight.
- Makes their minds up with ease and are prone to be opinionated.
- Prefers frequent setups over waiting for long-term setups to form.
- Has a basket of markets to sift through on the daily the night before.
- Not out to make huge trades.
- Likes to limit their focus to select short-term strikes.
This is a bit of a trader psyche or an overview from a hypothetical trader standpoint. If you find yourself
aligning with this thought process, or if it fits your personality or how you like to internalize price action,
then this might be a good foundation model to work with.

section_title: PURPOSE OF THE MODEL AND CORE SETUP
content:
,239
It's not meant to be a replacement to everything you think you've learned and then throw everything out the
window. You want to have something as a basis to build on, and that's what the premise is between the first
model and the last one of the 12 that I'm going to be doing in 2018. Again, it's just a suggestion to work
within so that way you go into the marketplace with your demo account and you engage price action with a
rule-based idea. Since this is the first one, it has the least in terms of rules, but it's still very, very good.
I'm hoping that you know what was shown in the High Probability Scalping Volumes 1 through 3, and this is
going to further enhance that with a mentorship perspective. The pattern setup that's going to be used for
this model is the Optimal Trade Entry in the New York Kill Zone, and we're focusing primarily on previous
daily range raids.

section_title: SETUP OVERVIEW AND PREVIOUS DAY RANGE DEFINITION
content:
,959
The setup overview:
- We're going to be buying intraday New York session bullish Optimal Trade Entries while anticipating a rally
to the previous day's High.
- We're going to be shorting intraday New York session bearish Optimal Trade Entries while anticipating a
decline to the previous day's low.
- Projecting 10, 20, or 30 pips beyond the previous day's range.
Targeted previous day is not limited to the calendar day before today, or whatever the trading day is that
you're executing, but rather any previous day inside the current 20-day IPA data range.

section_title: IDENTIFYING KEY LEVELS WITHIN THE 20-DAY IPA DATA RANGE
content:
,120
You can see here I have an example of this week's recording. I did some analysis, you guys saw that on the
forum, and this is more or less how I used the IPA data range just to form an opinion about what was going
to be attacked and how I numbered those days and how I defined the 20-day look-back period.
I'm zoomed in here a little bit. You can see obviously the highest of the last 20 days is right in here, and the
lowest of the last 20 days is right here. Obviously, this is a relatively equal low, so the liquidity pool is going
to be resting right below that.

section_title: UNDERSTANDING PREVIOUS DAILY RANGES AND PD ARRAYS
content:
,680
When we're looking for previous daily ranges (highs and lows), again, it's not simply what was yesterday's
high and low. What I'm referring to is the last 20 days range in terms of IPA data range. That's going to form
a basis of discount or premium.
So, when we're looking at the PD arrays inside the last 20 days, we're going to go through the list of the PD
array Matrix from premium to discount, and then also what we're going to be focusing primarily on is the
liquidity that rests below the lows.

section_title: PINPOINTING LIQUIDITY POOLS FROM KEY DAILY LOWS
content:
,280
I have all of the key lows from day one. That particular day, its low is recorded and extended across the
chart. Then day two, since it was the previous day, not Sunday (every candle has X above it, it's a Sunday
in here), so we're disregarding all Sundays when we're doing the PD arrays look-back in 20-day IPA data
ranges. It's the same with 40-day and 60-day; we're ignoring again the Sundays.
Then day three, we're using that low. Why that low? Because it's a swing low. And on day nine, we're using
that low because it was one of the strongest up moves, and it's the next previous day low from day one.
Again, do not be confused by my terminology saying that it's the previous day. It's a previous day inside of
the range defined by IPA. So in this model, we're only using the last 20 days. And then again, you can see
day 13, its low as well.
So we have a liquidity pool resting below day 13 and day nine, relatively equal lows, so we're anticipating a
run below those lows as a draw on liquidity. Disregard the liquidity void to the lower left; we're not drawing
any special attention to that right now when dealing with scalping.

section_title: REJECTION BLOCKS AND TURTLE SOUP
content:
,319
Behind that whole price structure, this is what led the analysis. The high was a rejection block ran through.
In other words, the rejection block is here. Price runs through it, making a higher high. It did not take the
wick out, we don't need that, but this is a turtle soup reversal pattern.
If you look at your dollar Index when we look at the bodies like this, you'll see the dollar Index was unwilling
to make a comparable lower low. So even though we didn't have that higher high seen here on Cable, we
did have it in the form of the rejection block right here.

section_title: MARKET STRUCTURE SHIFT, BREAKERS, AND BEARISH ORDER BLOCKS
content:
,560
The highs up close, that's what we're seeing ran out here. Then it's a subsequent breakdown. Then we
have the market structure shift right here. Price breaks down, trades back up into the breaker here. Then
we have a deeper move back into the breaker here also, and we're moving back into the range of these up
close candles here. So this will be one full order block in the bearish sense.
But the gray shaded area is focusing primarily on the bearish order block. Price trades up into it and then
starts to fall away, and then we come right back up into the bearish order block, which is the last up close
candle here inside the breaker as well. So we have some layering effects of all the PD arrays from a
premium standpoint.

section_title: WEEKLY HIGH PREDICTION AND LIQUIDITY RUNS
content:
,120
Price trades up into it on day one, that's what this day is here. And this is what I gave you on Sunday night.
That means this candle right here, while it was forming, I was telling you that the high was going to be right
in here for the week, and we would be looking for a run down below here as a potential run on liquidity.
Now, for a scalper, it's good to have these ideas because we're working off a daily time frame. Everything
I've taught you in the mentorship is being drawn into this, so I'm taking a great deal of liberty in expecting
that you know the things I'm showing you here.

section_title: MENTORSHIP CONCEPTS RECAP AND RANGE DYNAMICS
content:
,880
What a breaker is, what a rejection block is, turtle soup, market structure shift you all should know what
market structure shifts are. But then we have the liquidity pool resting below equal lows here, and then
obviously the bearish order block.
So while it's like this, we have ranges in here to work within. We have the highest high, the lowest low, and
right in here we're at or above equilibrium, and we're anticipating a move lower.

section_title: IPA DATA RANGE CONFIRMATION AND LOWER TIME FRAME ANALYSIS
content:
,560
So, going back to the IPA data range, you can see how price did in fact reach through all of these lows. And
then we're going to take a closer look; we're going to drop down into a lower time frame at these same
price levels or these red lines.
So we have an hourly chart here, so you can see how price has gravitated towards moving to and just
below these levels. So IPA is seeking liquidity below these known price points. So if we have them on our
chart, obviously we're going to be much more well-informed than the retail-minded traders or some of the
pseudo-professional traders.

section_title: PREVIOUS DAY LOW (PDL) DEFINITION AND PRE-WEEK ANALYSIS
content:
,720
I shared this chart also in the forum, and I want you to take note of how price did in fact seek liquidity below
previous day's lows. That's what the PDL stands for: previous day low. It doesn't mean and it's not defined
specifically as yesterday's low. What I'm saying is it's a previous daily low, but it's defined as such inside of
a range of the last 20 days. In this case here, we're looking at just till last Friday from the time of this
recording, February 28th, 2018.
So here's Friday's low. Here's Sunday's trading, and I gave you an analysis pre-week that this was going to
probably be our optimal trade entry selloff for the week. Now, I didn't know if it was going to be on Monday, I
don't always know that, but that's the area at which I was looking for to trade to.

section_title: PREMIUM BUILDUP AND SWING PROJECTIONS
content:
,360
If we know that we're likely to see this scenario unfold, then Monday or Tuesday or potentially Wednesday
the market could have rallied up to that price point. Since we saw all of this premium being built in, our eye
goes immediately to this low right here because this is our anchor or FC Chrome point.
The low up to this high, this is going to be used for swing projection at the last slide of this presentation. So
just remember that all this buildup here from Friday going into Monday's high, that's all being built as a
premium, and we'll look at that as a tool for grading our price swings and also looking for swing projections
on the week.

section_title: CONDITIONS AND MARKET STATES
content:
,920
Now let's get into the nuts and bolts about this. We're going to be defining Buy Programs. A buy program is
simply when we're looking to be a buyer. What are we defining as what makes it a buy program? In other
words, why are we only focusing on one side of the marketplace, in this case of being a buyer?
- Buying only when the daily has taken out a swing high in the last 20 days IPA data range and not in a
premium.
- Buying can be considered at equilibrium of the daily range of the 20-day IPA data range.
- Parabolic expansion runs will be above the equilibrium level of the 20-day IPA data range as it targets the
previous day high and its liquidity pool above it.

section_title: BUY PROGRAM EXAMPLE AND DAILY TIME FRAME
content:
,040
We have our range here: premium and discount. We have the lowest low here and the highest high here.
As price takes out this swing high here, right here, everything moving towards this level here could still be
used for buys. This would be a daily time frame; also, these are daily candles.

section_title: TIMING AND OPTIMAL TRADE ENTRY
content:
,720
The Buy Entry Process:
- When in a buy program, Monday through Wednesday is ideal buy days in New York session.
- Thursday can still be considered as long as the liquidity remains for low resistance liquidity runs. In other
words, if we haven't blown out the liquidity pool yet, Thursday still can be considered. But if I were trading
that day, and it's rare that I do, but if I did, my leverage would be dialed way back. I'd probably be doing
anywhere between 25% to 40% of what I would normally be trading in terms of leverage, simply because
we're so late in the week, long in the tooth on the range. So I wouldn't be worrying about too much of a, you
know, a gangbusters result or trying to get a big win.
- Between 7:00 a.m. to 11:00 a.m. New York time on a 5-minute chart, we're going to be looking for a
retracement lower against the London session momentum of the day using the bullish Optimal Trade Entry
pattern and keying off of the 62% level. Notice I refined it just to that level. We're not looking for 70.5, we're
not looking for 79% retracement level. We're looking specifically right at this 62% retracement level. But
now, since we're buying, we're going to add five pips for the spread for entry.

section_title: BUY ENTRY EXAMPLE
content:
,360
You see the example over here. We have our low right after the 7:00 a.m. time period. The market makes
an attempt to rally, and then we start seeing a retracement. As soon as we start seeing a retracement, we
run our FIB across the range. 62% retracement level right here plus five pips. That puts us in around here,
and price runs up, takes out the high here, and then continues to move higher.

section_title: LONG STOP LOSS PLACEMENT PROCESS
content:
,040
Long Stop Loss Placement Process:
- Using the low between 7:00 a.m. and 10:00 a.m. New York time as your foundation to the long entry, place
your protective sell stop at the low or five pips below it. That's right here.
- Do not move the protective stop until 20 pips has been scaled out of the position.
- Move the stop higher to lock in 5 to 10 pips after first scaling out, or price moves above your New York
session initial high (that means where you anchored your FIB prior to the trade entry).
- If prices run to your stop, do not take a re-entry on the trade.

section_title: LONG POSITION TARGETS AND SYMMETRICAL PRICE SWINGS
content:
,560
Long Position Targets:
- Take first scaling off just before returning to the initial high of the day or the high of the day.
- Take another scaling off at Target One on your FIB tool.
- Take another scaling at Target Two on your FIB tool.
- If news is due out late New York (post noon time, again New York time), leave a small portion on to see if
a symmetrical price swing can be reached.
- If it does ever intraday hit a symmetrical price swing, close all the position.
Now, there's going to be times where the market will continuously run, and you'll regret having closed
everything at the symmetrical price swing. Trust me, and I tell you, more times than not, it's better to do it
because it usually peters out and runs out of steam. Only a few instances out of a year you're going to see
how it just starts to go careening past the symmetrical price swing, unless we're in something obviously,
you know, very fast marketplace. It's not going to do that; it'll usually respect the symmetrical price swing, at
least in terms of capping the daily range, especially if time of day overlaps like London close or 2 to 3
o'clock in the afternoon with bonds closing.

section_title: CONDITIONS AND MARKET STATES
content:
,679
Now we're going to define the Sell Program.
- Shorting only when the daily has taken out a swing low in the last 20 days IPA data range and not in a
discount.
- Shorting can be considered at equilibrium of the daily range of the 20-day IPA data range.
- Parabolic expansion runs will be below the equilibrium level of the 20-day IPA data range as it targets the
previous day low and its liquidity pool below it.

section_title: SELL PROGRAM EXAMPLE
content:
,440
So we're looking at the example here, and again, this is Cable. We have that higher high in terms of the
bodies. We're defining the last 20 days here, and this is equilibrium. This is...
